content.content:
1 # Recipe Bottle 2 3 Recipe Bottle provides two independent container image capabilities: 4 5 - **[Foundry](#Foundry)**: orchestrate Google Cloud Build to produce and retain images in a private cloud registry — egress-locked builds with full supply-chain provenance 6 - **[Crucible](#Crucible)**: run untrusted containers behind enforced network isolation — DNS filtering and IP filtering — without modifying the workload image 7 8 > [!IMPORTANT] 9 > **Early-stage project — security review welcome in both domains** 10 > 11 > The [Foundry's](#Foundry) egress-locked Cloud Build configuration — including the SLSA attestation chain, build isolation, and digest-pinned toolchains — has not yet had broad independent review. 12 > 13 > The [Crucible](#Crucible) runtime containment — a multi-container apparatus where the workload runs unprivileged in a network namespace it does not control — has also not had broad review, particularly the iptables rules, privileged namespace setup, and network isolation enforcement. 14 > 15 > If you evaluate or deploy this, you are contributing to its hardening. Security-focused contributors and responsible disclosure are especially valued. 16 17 Recipe Bottle is a set of bash scripts designed for incorporation into arbitrary projects. The dependency footprint is deliberately narrow — `bash 3.2` and a handful of standard tools — with no Python runtime, no language-specific package manager, and no `gcloud` CLI. A small team can stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without specialized DevOps expertise. Recipe Bottle's goal is a workflow where every container image has a verified origin and a controlled version, running behind appropriate network safeguards. 18 19 **Project page**: https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle 20 21

22 23

24 25 ## Foundry 26 27 The [Foundry](#Foundry) orchestrates Google Cloud Build to produce container images with SLSA attestation, software bills of material, reproducible multi-architecture builds, and digest-pinned toolchains — so every image has a verifiable origin story. Builds run in an egress-locked configuration, drawing from upstream base images mirrored into a project-owned registry — a fixed, self-contained supply chain independent of third-party registry availability. 28 29 ### Foundry 30 31 Recipe Bottle's remote build orchestration system for producing, attesting, and distributing container images via Google Cloud Build and Google Artifact Registry. The [Foundry](#Foundry) encompasses [Depots](#Depot), [Vessels](#Vessel), [Hallmark](#Hallmark) tracking, and build definitions. Three [Vessel](#Vessel) modes determine how images enter the [Depot](#Depot): [Conjure](#Conjure) (egress-locked build from source with SLSA provenance), [Bind](#Bind) (digest-pinned upstream mirror), and [Graft](#Graft) (local push). Peer to [Crucible](#Crucible), which handles local runtime containment. 32 33 ### Vessel 34 35 A specification for a container image — built from source ([Conjure](#Conjure)), mirrored from upstream ([Bind](#Bind)), or pushed from local ([Graft](#Graft)). Each [Vessel](#Vessel) is a directory under `rbev-vessels/` containing at minimum an `rbrv.env` configuration file; [Conjure](#Conjure) [Vessels](#Vessel) also include a Dockerfile. The [Vessel](#Vessel) mode determines how the [Director](#Director) [Ordains](#Ordain) its [Hallmark](#Hallmark). 36 37 ### Hallmark 38 39 A specific build instance of a [Vessel](#Vessel), identified by timestamp. [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) are the unit of provenance tracking — each one records when and how the image was produced. Each [Hallmark](#Hallmark) produces three tagged artifacts in the registry: the container image (`-image`), the software bill of materials (`-about`), and the cryptographic attestation (`-vouch`). [Hallmark](#Hallmark) values are recorded into [Nameplate](#Nameplate) regime files to pin a [Crucible](#Crucible) to specific image versions. 40 41 ### Vouch 42 43 Cryptographic attestation proving a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) was built by trusted infrastructure. The [Vouch](#Vouch) verdict is mode-aware: [Conjure](#Conjure) builds receive full SLSA provenance verification, [Bind](#Bind) builds receive digest-pin verification, and [Graft](#Graft) builds receive a GRAFTED verdict with no provenance chain. The [Director](#Director) [Vouches](#Vouch) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) after [Tallying](#Tally) their build status. 44 45 ### Depot 46 47 The facility where container images are built and stored — a GCP project with an artifact registry and a storage bucket. The [Payor](#Payor) [Levies](#Levy) a [Depot](#Depot), and the [Governor](#Governor) administers access to it. Each [Depot](#Depot) operates as an independent supply-chain boundary with its own credentials, builds, and registry. 48 49 ### Ordain 50 51 Create a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) with full attestation — the production build operation. [Ordaining](#Ordain) is mode-aware: it [Conjures](#Conjure), [Binds](#Bind), or [Grafts](#Graft) depending on the [Vessel's](#Vessel) configuration. Each [Ordain](#Ordain) produces an image in the registry with associated provenance metadata. 52 53 ### Conjure 54 55 [Ordain](#Ordain) mode: Cloud Build creates the image from source. [Conjure](#Conjure) builds run in an egress-locked environment with digest-pinned toolchains, producing full SLSA attestation and SBOMs. This is the highest-trust build mode. 56 57 ### Bind 58 59 [Ordain](#Ordain) mode: mirror an upstream image pinned by digest. [Binding](#Bind) captures an external image at a specific digest into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. Trust is established through digest-pin verification rather than build provenance. 60 61 ### Graft 62 63 [Ordain](#Ordain) mode: push a locally-built image to the registry. [Grafting](#Graft) uploads a local image to GAR via docker push — no Cloud Build for the image itself, though about and [Vouch](#Vouch) metadata still run in Cloud Build. This is the lowest-trust mode (GRAFTED verdict). 64 65 ### Kludge 66 67 Build a [Vessel](#Vessel) image locally for fast iteration, without registry push. [Kludging](#Kludge) produces a local Docker image for development and testing without involving Cloud Build or the [Depot](#Depot). The resulting image can be used to [Charge](#Charge) a [Crucible](#Crucible) directly. 68 69 ### Enshrine 70 71 Mirror upstream base images into your [Depot's](#Depot) registry. [Enshrining](#Enshrine) ensures the build pipeline has a fixed, self-contained supply chain — builds draw from project-owned copies rather than depending on third-party registry availability at build time. 72 73 ### Reliquary 74 75 Co-versioned set of builder tool images (skopeo, docker, gcloud, syft) inscribed from upstream into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. Cloud Build jobs use [Reliquary](#Reliquary) images as step containers, ensuring builds run with known, project-owned toolchains rather than pulling tools from upstream at build time. The [Director](#Director) inscribes a [Reliquary](#Reliquary) before any [Ordain](#Ordain) or [Enshrine](#Enshrine) operation can run. 76 77 ### Pouch 78 79 Build context packaged as a FROM SCRATCH OCI image and pushed to the [Depot's](#Depot) registry before a Cloud Build job runs. The [Director](#Director) controls what enters the [Pouch](#Pouch) — Dockerfile, context files, build scripts — and the cloud receives only what the [Pouch](#Pouch) contains. This is the security boundary between workstation and build infrastructure. 80 81 ### Tethered 82 83 Build egress mode allowing public internet access during Cloud Build. [Tethered](#Tethered) builds pull base images from upstream registries at build time — simpler to set up, but dependent on upstream availability. Compare with [Airgap](#Airgap). 84 85 ### Airgap 86 87 Build egress mode with no public internet access during Cloud Build. [Airgap](#Airgap) builds draw all dependencies from [Enshrined](#Enshrine) images in the [Depot's](#Depot) registry — fully self-contained, independent of upstream availability. Requires [Enshrining](#Enshrine) base images before the first build. Compare with [Tethered](#Tethered). 88 89 ### Summon 90 91 Pull a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) image from the [Depot](#Depot) to your local machine. The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images for local use — the final step before a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) can be used in a [Crucible](#Crucible). 92 93 ### Plumb 94 95 Inspect an artifact's provenance — SBOM, build info, and [Vouch](#Vouch) chain. [Plumbing](#Plumb) provides full transparency into how an image was built and what it contains. Two views are available: full (SBOM, build info, Dockerfile) and compact (attestation summary). 96 97 ### Tally 98 99 Inventory [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) in the registry by health status. [Tallying](#Tally) shows which builds succeeded, which are pending, and which failed. The [Director](#Director) [Tallies](#Tally) before [Vouching](#Vouch) to confirm build completion. 100 101 ### Levy 102 103 Provision a new [Depot's](#Depot) GCP infrastructure. [Levying](#Levy) creates the GCP project, artifact registry, storage bucket, and build configuration. This is a [Payor](#Payor) operation that binds regime configuration to real cloud resources. 104 105 ### Payor 106 107 Owns the GCP project and funds it; authenticates via OAuth. The [Payor](#Payor) is the only role requiring manual Google Cloud Console interaction — establishing the project, configuring OAuth, and authorizing via browser flow. All other roles descend from credentials the [Payor's](#Payor) infrastructure creates. 108 109 ### Governor 110 111 Administers a [Depot](#Depot): creates service accounts, manages access. The [Governor](#Governor) is mantled by the [Payor](#Payor) and holds the administrative credential for the [Depot](#Depot). The [Governor](#Governor) [Knights](#Knight) [Directors](#Director) and [Charters](#Charter) [Retrievers](#Retriever). 112 113 ### Director 114 115 Builds and publishes [Vessel](#Vessel) images into a [Depot](#Depot). The [Director](#Director) [Ordains](#Ordain) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark), [Tallies](#Tally) build status, [Vouches](#Vouch) provenance, and manages the image lifecycle. Each [Director](#Director) credential is scoped to one [Depot](#Depot). 116 117 ### Retriever 118 119 Pulls and runs [Vessel](#Vessel) images from a [Depot](#Depot). The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images and [Plumbs](#Plumb) their provenance. This is the most constrained role — read-only access to the registry. 120 121 ### Charter 122 123 Create a [Retriever](#Retriever) service account — a [Governor](#Governor) operation. [Chartering](#Charter) provisions a new credential scoped to image pull access within a single [Depot](#Depot). 124 125 ### Knight 126 127 Create a [Director](#Director) service account — a [Governor](#Governor) operation. [Knighting](#Knight) provisions a new credential scoped to build and publish access within a single [Depot](#Depot). 128 129 ## Crucible 130 131 The distinctive case Recipe Bottle addresses is *running untrusted code*: third-party tooling, experimental packages, binaries with uncertain provenance. Containers excel at packaging known applications, but running unvetted code poses security risks that ordinary container deployment does not solve. Recipe Bottle assembles a [Crucible](#Crucible) — three cooperating containers where a [Sentry](#Sentry) enforces network policy via `iptables` and `dnsmasq` — without requiring modifications to existing container images. The [Bottle](#Bottle) container runs unmodified, in a network namespace prepared by a privileged [Pentacle](#Pentacle), with all egress flowing through the [Sentry](#Sentry) gateway. 132 133 ### Nameplate 134 135 Per-[Vessel](#Vessel) configuration tying a [Sentry](#Sentry) and [Bottle](#Bottle) together into a runnable [Crucible](#Crucible). The [Nameplate](#Nameplate) moniker (e.g. `tadmor`) identifies the unit across all operations. Each [Nameplate](#Nameplate) declares its [Vessel](#Vessel) selections, [Hallmark](#Hallmark) pins, and the network policy that the [Sentry](#Sentry) enforces. 136 137 ### Sentry 138 139 Security container enforcing network policies via `iptables` and `dnsmasq`. The [Sentry](#Sentry) applies two layers of egress policy: DNS-level filtering (only allowed domain names resolve) and IP-level filtering (only allowed CIDR ranges pass). A compromised [Bottle](#Bottle) cannot bypass either layer — the [Sentry](#Sentry) is the sole gateway between the [Bottle](#Bottle) and the outside network. 140 141 ### Pentacle 142 143 Privileged container establishing the network namespace shared with the [Bottle](#Bottle). The [Pentacle](#Pentacle) runs briefly with elevated privileges to create the network topology, then remains as the namespace anchor. Security policies are enforced from the first packet because the [Sentry](#Sentry) configures the namespace before the [Bottle](#Bottle) starts. 144 145 ### Bottle 146 147 Your workload container, running unmodified in a controlled network environment. The [Bottle](#Bottle) has no direct network access — all traffic routes through the [Sentry](#Sentry) gateway in a namespace prepared by the [Pentacle](#Pentacle). Any existing container image can run as a [Bottle](#Bottle) without modification. 148 149 ### Crucible 150 151 The [Sentry](#Sentry)/[Pentacle](#Pentacle)/[Bottle](#Bottle) triad running together as one unit for a [Nameplate](#Nameplate). The [Crucible](#Crucible) is the local safety orchestration — the apparatus that makes running untrusted code practical. [Charging](#Charge) starts all three containers; [Quenching](#Quench) stops and cleans them up. 152 153 ### Charge 154 155 Start the [Sentry](#Sentry)/[Pentacle](#Pentacle)/[Bottle](#Bottle) triad for a [Nameplate](#Nameplate). [Charging](#Charge) brings up the [Crucible](#Crucible) in dependency order: [Pentacle](#Pentacle) creates the namespace, [Sentry](#Sentry) configures policy, then the [Bottle](#Bottle) starts with its network already constrained. 156 157 ### Quench 158 159 Stop and clean up a [Charged](#Charge) [Nameplate's](#Nameplate) containers. [Quenching](#Quench) tears down the [Crucible](#Crucible) in reverse order and removes the network resources created during [Charging](#Charge). 160 161 ## How It Works 162 163 Recipe Bottle addresses two orthogonal complexity domains: the [Foundry](#Foundry) builds container images with verifiable provenance, and the [Crucible](#Crucible) runs untrusted images with enforced network isolation. The two compose but neither requires the other. 164 165 ### Image Management 166 167 Recipe Bottle builds container images on Google Cloud Build (GCB) and stores them in Google Artifact Registry (GAR): 168 169 - Isolated build environments using Google-curated Cloud Build builder images 170 - Multi-architecture support via `docker buildx` with binfmt emulation 171 - SLSA provenance attestation and verification 172 - Software Bills of Material (SBOM) for every build 173 - Full build transcripts captured as auxiliary metadata artifacts 174 - Upstream base images [Enshrined](#Enshrine) into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry, so builds do not depend on third-party registry availability at build time 175 - `gcloud` never runs on the workstation — REST calls via `curl` and `jq` drive all remote operations, and the Google-supplied `gcloud` binary is confined to Cloud Build step containers on the server side 176 177 ### Crucible Orchestration 178 179 For running containers with network services, Recipe Bottle orchestrates three containers working together: 180 181 - **[Sentry](#Sentry)** — enforces network security policies via `iptables` and `dnsmasq` 182 - **[Pentacle](#Pentacle)** — establishes a privileged network namespace and shares it with the [Bottle](#Bottle) 183 - **[Bottle](#Bottle)** — your workload container, running unmodified in a controlled network environment 184 185 This ensures security policies are enforced from the first packet, and the [Bottle](#Bottle) container experiences only a functional path to its [Sentry](#Sentry) gateway. 186 187 The [Sentry](#Sentry) applies two layers of egress policy: `dnsmasq` answers DNS queries only for explicitly allowed names, and `iptables` permits outbound IP traffic only to allowed CIDR ranges. The two layers combine into an enforced allowlist that a compromised or misbehaving [Bottle](#Bottle) cannot bypass — neither by DNS-based exfiltration nor by direct IP connection to an unapproved destination. Each [Crucible's](#Crucible) allowlist is declared in a [Nameplate](#Nameplate) regime file alongside the [Sentry](#Sentry) and [Bottle](#Bottle) [Vessel](#Vessel) selections, making network policy a reviewable artifact of the configuration rather than an implicit runtime behavior. 188 189 ### Project Direction 190 191 **[Foundry](#Foundry).** Egress lockdown is implemented via a dual-pool Cloud Build architecture. VPC Service Controls and cosign signing are evaluated and deferred until organizational policy or external distribution triggers them. 192 193 **Runtime.** Docker on Linux is the first-class runtime; rootless Podman and macOS-native workflows are deferred pending user demand. One known weakness: when allowed domains are CDN-hosted (e.g. Cloudflare), the [Sentry's](#Sentry) CIDR allowlist becomes coarse — DNS-level gating remains precise, but IP-level gating is porous across shared CDN ranges. 194 195 **Networking.** [Bottle](#Bottle)-to-[Bottle](#Bottle) communication is feasible under the current [Sentry](#Sentry) model but not implemented; waiting for a concrete use case. 196 197 ## Prerequisites 198 199 - macOS or Linux workstation 200 - `bash` (3.2+), `git`, `curl`, `openssh`, `jq`, `openssl` 201 - `docker` (container runtime) 202 - A Google Cloud account with billing enabled (credit card required for verification; free tier is sufficient to start) 203 204 ## Using the CLI 205 206 ### Tabtarget 207 208 Lightweight shell script in the `tt/` directory serving as the entry point for a single CLI operation. [Tabtargets](#Tabtarget) are named `{colophon}.{frontispiece}.sh` — the colophon routes to the right module, the frontispiece describes what it does. Tab completion narrows by prefix: `tt/rbw-` shows all Recipe Bottle operations. The project's `CLAUDE.md` provides a complete command reference; the CLI's interactive walkthroughs are the authoritative procedure source. 209 210 ## Setup 211 212 Recipe Bottle uses a role-based security model with four roles, each building on the previous: 213 214 | Role | Authenticates via | Purpose | 215 |------|-------------------|---------| 216 | [**Payor**](#Payor) | OAuth (browser flow) | Creates/funds GCP infrastructure, manages [Governor](#Governor) lifecycle | 217 | [**Governor**](#Governor) | Service account credential | Administers [Director](#Director) and [Retriever](#Retriever) credentials within a [Depot](#Depot) | 218 | [**Director**](#Director) | Service account credential | Submits builds, manages images, verifies provenance | 219 | [**Retriever**](#Retriever) | Service account credential | Pulls images for local use | 220 221 The [Payor](#Payor) stands apart — it requires manual Google Cloud Console work and OAuth authentication. All downstream roles authenticate via credential files, enabling full automation. 222 223 ### Establishment and Provisioning 224 225 The [Payor](#Payor) begins by creating a GCP project and configuring an OAuth consent screen through the Google Cloud Console. After downloading the OAuth client credentials, the [Payor](#Payor) installs them via a browser authorization flow — the resulting refresh token is stored locally with restrictive permissions and does not need to be repeated. 226 227 With [Payor](#Payor) credentials in place, the [Payor](#Payor) [Levies](#Levy) a [Depot](#Depot), provisioning it with build infrastructure, artifact registry, and secrets storage. The [Payor](#Payor) then mantles a [Governor](#Governor) service account to administer the [Depot](#Depot). 228 229 ### Credential Distribution 230 231 The [Governor](#Governor) creates downstream credentials: [Knighting](#Knight) a [Director](#Director) for build operations and [Chartering](#Charter) a [Retriever](#Retriever) for image pull access. Each credential is scoped to a single role within a single [Depot](#Depot). 232 233 ### Build and Retrieve 234 235 The [Director](#Director) [Ordains](#Ordain) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) for each [Vessel](#Vessel) — [Conjuring](#Conjure) from source, [Binding](#Bind) from upstream, or [Grafting](#Graft) from local builds. After builds complete, the [Director](#Director) [Tallies](#Tally) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) by health status and [Vouches](#Vouch) their provenance. [Hallmark](#Hallmark) values from the [Tally](#Tally) are recorded into [Nameplate](#Nameplate) regime files, completing the chain from build to runtime. 236 237 The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images locally for use. 238 239 ## Day-to-Day Operations 240 241 To run a workload, [Charge](#Charge) the [Crucible](#Crucible) for a [Nameplate](#Nameplate). This starts the [Sentry](#Sentry), [Pentacle](#Pentacle), and [Bottle](#Bottle) containers together — the [Bottle](#Bottle) is ready for interactive use immediately. 242 243 For diagnostics, shell into the [Bottle](#Bottle) or the [Sentry](#Sentry), or observe network traffic across the [Crucible's](#Crucible) containers. When finished, [Quench](#Quench) the [Crucible](#Crucible) to stop and clean up all three containers. 244 245 To inspect an image's supply chain, [Plumb](#Plumb) its provenance — the full view shows the SBOM, build info, and Dockerfile; the compact view summarizes the attestation chain. 246 247 ## Credential Safety 248 249 All credential files require `600` permissions and must never be committed to version control. 250 251 | Credential | Location | Created during | 252 |------------|----------|----------------| 253 | [Payor](#Payor) OAuth | `~/.rbw/rbro.env` | [Payor](#Payor) installation | 254 | [Governor](#Governor) | `RBRR_SECRETS_DIR/governor/rbra.env` | [Governor](#Governor) mantling | 255 | [Director](#Director) | `RBRR_SECRETS_DIR/director/rbra.env` | [Director](#Director) [Knighting](#Knight) | 256 | [Retriever](#Retriever) | `RBRR_SECRETS_DIR/retriever/rbra.env` | [Retriever](#Retriever) [Chartering](#Charter) | 257 258 Each credential file is scoped to one role within one [Depot](#Depot) and cannot operate outside its designation. 259 260 ## Configuration 261 262 ### Regime 263 264 Structured configuration with typed validation. Each [Regime](#Regime) is a set of environment variables in an `.env` file with a render command (display current values) and a validate command (check correctness). Recipe Bottle uses [Regimes](#Regime) for everything from [Depot](#Depot) identity to [Vessel](#Vessel) build definitions to developer workstation paths. 265 266 ### User-Configured Regimes 267 268 | Regime | File | Purpose | 269 |--------|------|---------| 270 | RBRP | `.rbk/rbrp.env` | [Payor](#Payor) GCP project identity | 271 | RBRR | `.rbk/rbrr.env` | [Depot](#Depot) project, region, build config | 272 | RBRN | `.rbk/*/rbrn.env` | Per-[Vessel](#Vessel): runtime, [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) | 273 | RBRV | [Vessel](#Vessel) dirs | Container image build definitions | 274 | RBRS | station file | Developer machine paths (not in git) | 275 276 ### Managed Regimes (generated by operations) 277 278 | Regime | Purpose | Generated during | 279 |--------|---------|-----------------| 280 | RBRO | OAuth refresh token | [Payor](#Payor) installation | 281 | RBRA | Service account credentials | Mantling, [Knighting](#Knight), [Chartering](#Charter) | 282 283 ### BUK Base Regimes 284 285 | Regime | File | Purpose | 286 |--------|------|---------| 287 | BURC | `.buk/burc.env` | Project structure ([Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) dir, tools dir) | 288 | BURS | `../station-files/burs.env` | Developer machine (log dir). Not in git. | 289 290 ## Vessels and Nameplates 291 292 **[Vessels](#Vessel)** are build definitions — each is a directory under `rbev-vessels/`: 293 294 ``` 295 rbev-vessels/ 296 ├── rbev-sentry-debian-slim/ # Sentry vessel (conjure — built from source) 297 │ ├── Dockerfile 298 │ └── rbrv.env 299 ├── rbev-bottle-ifrit/ # Ifrit bottle vessel (conjure — built from source) 300 │ ├── Dockerfile 301 │ └── rbrv.env 302 └── rbev-bottle-plantuml/ # PlantUML server (bind — upstream image pinned by digest) 303 └── rbrv.env 304 ``` 305 306 [Conjure](#Conjure) [Vessels](#Vessel) have a Dockerfile and are built by Cloud Build. [Bind](#Bind) [Vessels](#Vessel) (like `rbev-bottle-plantuml`) pin an external image by digest in `rbrv.env` — no Dockerfile, no build step. [Graft](#Graft) [Vessels](#Vessel) push a locally-built image to GAR via docker push — no Cloud Build for the image, but about and [Vouch](#Vouch) still run in Cloud Build. The same [Ordain](#Ordain) operation handles all three: it detects the [Vessel](#Vessel) mode and triggers a Cloud Build ([Conjure](#Conjure)), mirrors from upstream ([Bind](#Bind)), or pushes a local image ([Graft](#Graft)). Trust hierarchy: [Conjure](#Conjure) has full SLSA provenance, [Bind](#Bind) has digest-pin verification, [Graft](#Graft) has no provenance chain (GRAFTED verdict). 307 308 **[Nameplates](#Nameplate)** tie [Vessels](#Vessel) together into a runnable [Crucible](#Crucible). The [Nameplate](#Nameplate) moniker (e.g. `tadmor`) identifies the unit across all operations: 309 310 ``` 311 .rbk/tadmor/rbrn.env # Maps tadmor → rbev-sentry-debian-slim + rbev-bottle-ifrit 312 ``` 313 314 [Charging](#Charge) `tadmor` starts the [Crucible](#Crucible) defined by the `tadmor` [Nameplate](#Nameplate), which selects its [Sentry](#Sentry) and [Bottle](#Bottle) [Vessel](#Vessel) images. 315 316 ## Testing 317 318 Run test fixtures sequentially — they share regime state and container namespaces. Never run fixtures in parallel. 319 320 Two qualification gates exercise the full system: a fast qualify checks tabtarget structure, colophon integrity, and [Nameplate](#Nameplate) health; a release qualify adds shellcheck analysis and the complete test suite. 321 322 ## Recovery 323 324 - **Lost OAuth credentials**: Download a fresh JSON key from Google Cloud Console and re-run the [Payor](#Payor) installation 325 - **Expired tokens**: Run the [Payor](#Payor) refresh operation 326 - **Compromised [Governor](#Governor)**: Re-mantle the [Governor](#Governor) (replaces the service account, invalidates the old credential) 327 - **Compromised [Director](#Director)/[Retriever](#Retriever)**: Forfeit the compromised account, then re-[Knight](#Knight) or re-[Charter](#Charter) 328 - **Lost [Nameplate](#Nameplate) values**: Re-[Tally](#Tally) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) to retrieve values from the registry 329 - **Build timeout or failure**: [Tally](#Tally) to check build status, review logs in the GCP Console for the [Depot](#Depot) project 330 331 ## Architecture 332 333 ``` 334 Project Root/ 335 ├── .buk/ # BUK launcher directory + BURC config 336 ├── .rbk/ # Recipe Bottle config regimes 337 ├── tt/ # TabTargets — all CLI commands live here 338 ├── Tools/ 339 │ ├── buk/ # Bash Utility Kit (portable CLI infrastructure) 340 │ └── rbk/ # Recipe Bottle Kit (domain logic) 341 └── rbev-vessels/ # Vessel definitions (Dockerfile + rbrv.env per vessel) 342 ``` 343 344 ## Claude Code 345 346 If you use [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code), the project includes a `CLAUDE.md` with a full command reference table, glossary, and conventions for AI-assisted development. 347 348 ## License 349 350 Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc. 351 352 Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0. 353

file.content:
# Recipe Bottle Recipe Bottle provides two independent container image capabilities: - **[Foundry](#Foundry)**: orchestrate Google Cloud Build to produce and retain images in a private cloud registry — egress-locked builds with full supply-chain provenance - **[Crucible](#Crucible)**: run untrusted containers behind enforced network isolation — DNS filtering and IP filtering — without modifying the workload image > [!IMPORTANT] > **Early-stage project — security review welcome in both domains** > > The [Foundry's](#Foundry) egress-locked Cloud Build configuration — including the SLSA attestation chain, build isolation, and digest-pinned toolchains — has not yet had broad independent review. > > The [Crucible](#Crucible) runtime containment — a multi-container apparatus where the workload runs unprivileged in a network namespace it does not control — has also not had broad review, particularly the iptables rules, privileged namespace setup, and network isolation enforcement. > > If you evaluate or deploy this, you are contributing to its hardening. Security-focused contributors and responsible disclosure are especially valued. Recipe Bottle is a set of bash scripts designed for incorporation into arbitrary projects. The dependency footprint is deliberately narrow — `bash 3.2` and a handful of standard tools — with no Python runtime, no language-specific package manager, and no `gcloud` CLI. A small team can stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without specialized DevOps expertise. Recipe Bottle's goal is a workflow where every container image has a verified origin and a controlled version, running behind appropriate network safeguards. **Project page**: https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle

## Foundry The [Foundry](#Foundry) orchestrates Google Cloud Build to produce container images with SLSA attestation, software bills of material, reproducible multi-architecture builds, and digest-pinned toolchains — so every image has a verifiable origin story. Builds run in an egress-locked configuration, drawing from upstream base images mirrored into a project-owned registry — a fixed, self-contained supply chain independent of third-party registry availability. ### Foundry Recipe Bottle's remote build orchestration system for producing, attesting, and distributing container images via Google Cloud Build and Google Artifact Registry. The [Foundry](#Foundry) encompasses [Depots](#Depot), [Vessels](#Vessel), [Hallmark](#Hallmark) tracking, and build definitions. Three [Vessel](#Vessel) modes determine how images enter the [Depot](#Depot): [Conjure](#Conjure) (egress-locked build from source with SLSA provenance), [Bind](#Bind) (digest-pinned upstream mirror), and [Graft](#Graft) (local push). Peer to [Crucible](#Crucible), which handles local runtime containment. ### Vessel A specification for a container image — built from source ([Conjure](#Conjure)), mirrored from upstream ([Bind](#Bind)), or pushed from local ([Graft](#Graft)). Each [Vessel](#Vessel) is a directory under `rbev-vessels/` containing at minimum an `rbrv.env` configuration file; [Conjure](#Conjure) [Vessels](#Vessel) also include a Dockerfile. The [Vessel](#Vessel) mode determines how the [Director](#Director) [Ordains](#Ordain) its [Hallmark](#Hallmark). ### Hallmark A specific build instance of a [Vessel](#Vessel), identified by timestamp. [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) are the unit of provenance tracking — each one records when and how the image was produced. Each [Hallmark](#Hallmark) produces three tagged artifacts in the registry: the container image (`-image`), the software bill of materials (`-about`), and the cryptographic attestation (`-vouch`). [Hallmark](#Hallmark) values are recorded into [Nameplate](#Nameplate) regime files to pin a [Crucible](#Crucible) to specific image versions. ### Vouch Cryptographic attestation proving a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) was built by trusted infrastructure. The [Vouch](#Vouch) verdict is mode-aware: [Conjure](#Conjure) builds receive full SLSA provenance verification, [Bind](#Bind) builds receive digest-pin verification, and [Graft](#Graft) builds receive a GRAFTED verdict with no provenance chain. The [Director](#Director) [Vouches](#Vouch) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) after [Tallying](#Tally) their build status. ### Depot The facility where container images are built and stored — a GCP project with an artifact registry and a storage bucket. The [Payor](#Payor) [Levies](#Levy) a [Depot](#Depot), and the [Governor](#Governor) administers access to it. Each [Depot](#Depot) operates as an independent supply-chain boundary with its own credentials, builds, and registry. ### Ordain Create a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) with full attestation — the production build operation. [Ordaining](#Ordain) is mode-aware: it [Conjures](#Conjure), [Binds](#Bind), or [Grafts](#Graft) depending on the [Vessel's](#Vessel) configuration. Each [Ordain](#Ordain) produces an image in the registry with associated provenance metadata. ### Conjure [Ordain](#Ordain) mode: Cloud Build creates the image from source. [Conjure](#Conjure) builds run in an egress-locked environment with digest-pinned toolchains, producing full SLSA attestation and SBOMs. This is the highest-trust build mode. ### Bind [Ordain](#Ordain) mode: mirror an upstream image pinned by digest. [Binding](#Bind) captures an external image at a specific digest into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. Trust is established through digest-pin verification rather than build provenance. ### Graft [Ordain](#Ordain) mode: push a locally-built image to the registry. [Grafting](#Graft) uploads a local image to GAR via docker push — no Cloud Build for the image itself, though about and [Vouch](#Vouch) metadata still run in Cloud Build. This is the lowest-trust mode (GRAFTED verdict). ### Kludge Build a [Vessel](#Vessel) image locally for fast iteration, without registry push. [Kludging](#Kludge) produces a local Docker image for development and testing without involving Cloud Build or the [Depot](#Depot). The resulting image can be used to [Charge](#Charge) a [Crucible](#Crucible) directly. ### Enshrine Mirror upstream base images into your [Depot's](#Depot) registry. [Enshrining](#Enshrine) ensures the build pipeline has a fixed, self-contained supply chain — builds draw from project-owned copies rather than depending on third-party registry availability at build time. ### Reliquary Co-versioned set of builder tool images (skopeo, docker, gcloud, syft) inscribed from upstream into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. Cloud Build jobs use [Reliquary](#Reliquary) images as step containers, ensuring builds run with known, project-owned toolchains rather than pulling tools from upstream at build time. The [Director](#Director) inscribes a [Reliquary](#Reliquary) before any [Ordain](#Ordain) or [Enshrine](#Enshrine) operation can run. ### Pouch Build context packaged as a FROM SCRATCH OCI image and pushed to the [Depot's](#Depot) registry before a Cloud Build job runs. The [Director](#Director) controls what enters the [Pouch](#Pouch) — Dockerfile, context files, build scripts — and the cloud receives only what the [Pouch](#Pouch) contains. This is the security boundary between workstation and build infrastructure. ### Tethered Build egress mode allowing public internet access during Cloud Build. [Tethered](#Tethered) builds pull base images from upstream registries at build time — simpler to set up, but dependent on upstream availability. Compare with [Airgap](#Airgap). ### Airgap Build egress mode with no public internet access during Cloud Build. [Airgap](#Airgap) builds draw all dependencies from [Enshrined](#Enshrine) images in the [Depot's](#Depot) registry — fully self-contained, independent of upstream availability. Requires [Enshrining](#Enshrine) base images before the first build. Compare with [Tethered](#Tethered). ### Summon Pull a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) image from the [Depot](#Depot) to your local machine. The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images for local use — the final step before a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) can be used in a [Crucible](#Crucible). ### Plumb Inspect an artifact's provenance — SBOM, build info, and [Vouch](#Vouch) chain. [Plumbing](#Plumb) provides full transparency into how an image was built and what it contains. Two views are available: full (SBOM, build info, Dockerfile) and compact (attestation summary). ### Tally Inventory [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) in the registry by health status. [Tallying](#Tally) shows which builds succeeded, which are pending, and which failed. The [Director](#Director) [Tallies](#Tally) before [Vouching](#Vouch) to confirm build completion. ### Levy Provision a new [Depot's](#Depot) GCP infrastructure. [Levying](#Levy) creates the GCP project, artifact registry, storage bucket, and build configuration. This is a [Payor](#Payor) operation that binds regime configuration to real cloud resources. ### Payor Owns the GCP project and funds it; authenticates via OAuth. The [Payor](#Payor) is the only role requiring manual Google Cloud Console interaction — establishing the project, configuring OAuth, and authorizing via browser flow. All other roles descend from credentials the [Payor's](#Payor) infrastructure creates. ### Governor Administers a [Depot](#Depot): creates service accounts, manages access. The [Governor](#Governor) is mantled by the [Payor](#Payor) and holds the administrative credential for the [Depot](#Depot). The [Governor](#Governor) [Knights](#Knight) [Directors](#Director) and [Charters](#Charter) [Retrievers](#Retriever). ### Director Builds and publishes [Vessel](#Vessel) images into a [Depot](#Depot). The [Director](#Director) [Ordains](#Ordain) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark), [Tallies](#Tally) build status, [Vouches](#Vouch) provenance, and manages the image lifecycle. Each [Director](#Director) credential is scoped to one [Depot](#Depot). ### Retriever Pulls and runs [Vessel](#Vessel) images from a [Depot](#Depot). The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images and [Plumbs](#Plumb) their provenance. This is the most constrained role — read-only access to the registry. ### Charter Create a [Retriever](#Retriever) service account — a [Governor](#Governor) operation. [Chartering](#Charter) provisions a new credential scoped to image pull access within a single [Depot](#Depot). ### Knight Create a [Director](#Director) service account — a [Governor](#Governor) operation. [Knighting](#Knight) provisions a new credential scoped to build and publish access within a single [Depot](#Depot). ## Crucible The distinctive case Recipe Bottle addresses is *running untrusted code*: third-party tooling, experimental packages, binaries with uncertain provenance. Containers excel at packaging known applications, but running unvetted code poses security risks that ordinary container deployment does not solve. Recipe Bottle assembles a [Crucible](#Crucible) — three cooperating containers where a [Sentry](#Sentry) enforces network policy via `iptables` and `dnsmasq` — without requiring modifications to existing container images. The [Bottle](#Bottle) container runs unmodified, in a network namespace prepared by a privileged [Pentacle](#Pentacle), with all egress flowing through the [Sentry](#Sentry) gateway. ### Nameplate Per-[Vessel](#Vessel) configuration tying a [Sentry](#Sentry) and [Bottle](#Bottle) together into a runnable [Crucible](#Crucible). The [Nameplate](#Nameplate) moniker (e.g. `tadmor`) identifies the unit across all operations. Each [Nameplate](#Nameplate) declares its [Vessel](#Vessel) selections, [Hallmark](#Hallmark) pins, and the network policy that the [Sentry](#Sentry) enforces. ### Sentry Security container enforcing network policies via `iptables` and `dnsmasq`. The [Sentry](#Sentry) applies two layers of egress policy: DNS-level filtering (only allowed domain names resolve) and IP-level filtering (only allowed CIDR ranges pass). A compromised [Bottle](#Bottle) cannot bypass either layer — the [Sentry](#Sentry) is the sole gateway between the [Bottle](#Bottle) and the outside network. ### Pentacle Privileged container establishing the network namespace shared with the [Bottle](#Bottle). The [Pentacle](#Pentacle) runs briefly with elevated privileges to create the network topology, then remains as the namespace anchor. Security policies are enforced from the first packet because the [Sentry](#Sentry) configures the namespace before the [Bottle](#Bottle) starts. ### Bottle Your workload container, running unmodified in a controlled network environment. The [Bottle](#Bottle) has no direct network access — all traffic routes through the [Sentry](#Sentry) gateway in a namespace prepared by the [Pentacle](#Pentacle). Any existing container image can run as a [Bottle](#Bottle) without modification. ### Crucible The [Sentry](#Sentry)/[Pentacle](#Pentacle)/[Bottle](#Bottle) triad running together as one unit for a [Nameplate](#Nameplate). The [Crucible](#Crucible) is the local safety orchestration — the apparatus that makes running untrusted code practical. [Charging](#Charge) starts all three containers; [Quenching](#Quench) stops and cleans them up. ### Charge Start the [Sentry](#Sentry)/[Pentacle](#Pentacle)/[Bottle](#Bottle) triad for a [Nameplate](#Nameplate). [Charging](#Charge) brings up the [Crucible](#Crucible) in dependency order: [Pentacle](#Pentacle) creates the namespace, [Sentry](#Sentry) configures policy, then the [Bottle](#Bottle) starts with its network already constrained. ### Quench Stop and clean up a [Charged](#Charge) [Nameplate's](#Nameplate) containers. [Quenching](#Quench) tears down the [Crucible](#Crucible) in reverse order and removes the network resources created during [Charging](#Charge). ## How It Works Recipe Bottle addresses two orthogonal complexity domains: the [Foundry](#Foundry) builds container images with verifiable provenance, and the [Crucible](#Crucible) runs untrusted images with enforced network isolation. The two compose but neither requires the other. ### Image Management Recipe Bottle builds container images on Google Cloud Build (GCB) and stores them in Google Artifact Registry (GAR): - Isolated build environments using Google-curated Cloud Build builder images - Multi-architecture support via `docker buildx` with binfmt emulation - SLSA provenance attestation and verification - Software Bills of Material (SBOM) for every build - Full build transcripts captured as auxiliary metadata artifacts - Upstream base images [Enshrined](#Enshrine) into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry, so builds do not depend on third-party registry availability at build time - `gcloud` never runs on the workstation — REST calls via `curl` and `jq` drive all remote operations, and the Google-supplied `gcloud` binary is confined to Cloud Build step containers on the server side ### Crucible Orchestration For running containers with network services, Recipe Bottle orchestrates three containers working together: - **[Sentry](#Sentry)** — enforces network security policies via `iptables` and `dnsmasq` - **[Pentacle](#Pentacle)** — establishes a privileged network namespace and shares it with the [Bottle](#Bottle) - **[Bottle](#Bottle)** — your workload container, running unmodified in a controlled network environment This ensures security policies are enforced from the first packet, and the [Bottle](#Bottle) container experiences only a functional path to its [Sentry](#Sentry) gateway. The [Sentry](#Sentry) applies two layers of egress policy: `dnsmasq` answers DNS queries only for explicitly allowed names, and `iptables` permits outbound IP traffic only to allowed CIDR ranges. The two layers combine into an enforced allowlist that a compromised or misbehaving [Bottle](#Bottle) cannot bypass — neither by DNS-based exfiltration nor by direct IP connection to an unapproved destination. Each [Crucible's](#Crucible) allowlist is declared in a [Nameplate](#Nameplate) regime file alongside the [Sentry](#Sentry) and [Bottle](#Bottle) [Vessel](#Vessel) selections, making network policy a reviewable artifact of the configuration rather than an implicit runtime behavior. ### Project Direction **[Foundry](#Foundry).** Egress lockdown is implemented via a dual-pool Cloud Build architecture. VPC Service Controls and cosign signing are evaluated and deferred until organizational policy or external distribution triggers them. **Runtime.** Docker on Linux is the first-class runtime; rootless Podman and macOS-native workflows are deferred pending user demand. One known weakness: when allowed domains are CDN-hosted (e.g. Cloudflare), the [Sentry's](#Sentry) CIDR allowlist becomes coarse — DNS-level gating remains precise, but IP-level gating is porous across shared CDN ranges. **Networking.** [Bottle](#Bottle)-to-[Bottle](#Bottle) communication is feasible under the current [Sentry](#Sentry) model but not implemented; waiting for a concrete use case. ## Prerequisites - macOS or Linux workstation - `bash` (3.2+), `git`, `curl`, `openssh`, `jq`, `openssl` - `docker` (container runtime) - A Google Cloud account with billing enabled (credit card required for verification; free tier is sufficient to start) ## Using the CLI ### Tabtarget Lightweight shell script in the `tt/` directory serving as the entry point for a single CLI operation. [Tabtargets](#Tabtarget) are named `{colophon}.{frontispiece}.sh` — the colophon routes to the right module, the frontispiece describes what it does. Tab completion narrows by prefix: `tt/rbw-` shows all Recipe Bottle operations. The project's `CLAUDE.md` provides a complete command reference; the CLI's interactive walkthroughs are the authoritative procedure source. ## Setup Recipe Bottle uses a role-based security model with four roles, each building on the previous: | Role | Authenticates via | Purpose | |------|-------------------|---------| | [**Payor**](#Payor) | OAuth (browser flow) | Creates/funds GCP infrastructure, manages [Governor](#Governor) lifecycle | | [**Governor**](#Governor) | Service account credential | Administers [Director](#Director) and [Retriever](#Retriever) credentials within a [Depot](#Depot) | | [**Director**](#Director) | Service account credential | Submits builds, manages images, verifies provenance | | [**Retriever**](#Retriever) | Service account credential | Pulls images for local use | The [Payor](#Payor) stands apart — it requires manual Google Cloud Console work and OAuth authentication. All downstream roles authenticate via credential files, enabling full automation. ### Establishment and Provisioning The [Payor](#Payor) begins by creating a GCP project and configuring an OAuth consent screen through the Google Cloud Console. After downloading the OAuth client credentials, the [Payor](#Payor) installs them via a browser authorization flow — the resulting refresh token is stored locally with restrictive permissions and does not need to be repeated. With [Payor](#Payor) credentials in place, the [Payor](#Payor) [Levies](#Levy) a [Depot](#Depot), provisioning it with build infrastructure, artifact registry, and secrets storage. The [Payor](#Payor) then mantles a [Governor](#Governor) service account to administer the [Depot](#Depot). ### Credential Distribution The [Governor](#Governor) creates downstream credentials: [Knighting](#Knight) a [Director](#Director) for build operations and [Chartering](#Charter) a [Retriever](#Retriever) for image pull access. Each credential is scoped to a single role within a single [Depot](#Depot). ### Build and Retrieve The [Director](#Director) [Ordains](#Ordain) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) for each [Vessel](#Vessel) — [Conjuring](#Conjure) from source, [Binding](#Bind) from upstream, or [Grafting](#Graft) from local builds. After builds complete, the [Director](#Director) [Tallies](#Tally) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) by health status and [Vouches](#Vouch) their provenance. [Hallmark](#Hallmark) values from the [Tally](#Tally) are recorded into [Nameplate](#Nameplate) regime files, completing the chain from build to runtime. The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images locally for use. ## Day-to-Day Operations To run a workload, [Charge](#Charge) the [Crucible](#Crucible) for a [Nameplate](#Nameplate). This starts the [Sentry](#Sentry), [Pentacle](#Pentacle), and [Bottle](#Bottle) containers together — the [Bottle](#Bottle) is ready for interactive use immediately. For diagnostics, shell into the [Bottle](#Bottle) or the [Sentry](#Sentry), or observe network traffic across the [Crucible's](#Crucible) containers. When finished, [Quench](#Quench) the [Crucible](#Crucible) to stop and clean up all three containers. To inspect an image's supply chain, [Plumb](#Plumb) its provenance — the full view shows the SBOM, build info, and Dockerfile; the compact view summarizes the attestation chain. ## Credential Safety All credential files require `600` permissions and must never be committed to version control. | Credential | Location | Created during | |------------|----------|----------------| | [Payor](#Payor) OAuth | `~/.rbw/rbro.env` | [Payor](#Payor) installation | | [Governor](#Governor) | `RBRR_SECRETS_DIR/governor/rbra.env` | [Governor](#Governor) mantling | | [Director](#Director) | `RBRR_SECRETS_DIR/director/rbra.env` | [Director](#Director) [Knighting](#Knight) | | [Retriever](#Retriever) | `RBRR_SECRETS_DIR/retriever/rbra.env` | [Retriever](#Retriever) [Chartering](#Charter) | Each credential file is scoped to one role within one [Depot](#Depot) and cannot operate outside its designation. ## Configuration ### Regime Structured configuration with typed validation. Each [Regime](#Regime) is a set of environment variables in an `.env` file with a render command (display current values) and a validate command (check correctness). Recipe Bottle uses [Regimes](#Regime) for everything from [Depot](#Depot) identity to [Vessel](#Vessel) build definitions to developer workstation paths. ### User-Configured Regimes | Regime | File | Purpose | |--------|------|---------| | RBRP | `.rbk/rbrp.env` | [Payor](#Payor) GCP project identity | | RBRR | `.rbk/rbrr.env` | [Depot](#Depot) project, region, build config | | RBRN | `.rbk/*/rbrn.env` | Per-[Vessel](#Vessel): runtime, [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) | | RBRV | [Vessel](#Vessel) dirs | Container image build definitions | | RBRS | station file | Developer machine paths (not in git) | ### Managed Regimes (generated by operations) | Regime | Purpose | Generated during | |--------|---------|-----------------| | RBRO | OAuth refresh token | [Payor](#Payor) installation | | RBRA | Service account credentials | Mantling, [Knighting](#Knight), [Chartering](#Charter) | ### BUK Base Regimes | Regime | File | Purpose | |--------|------|---------| | BURC | `.buk/burc.env` | Project structure ([Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) dir, tools dir) | | BURS | `../station-files/burs.env` | Developer machine (log dir). Not in git. | ## Vessels and Nameplates **[Vessels](#Vessel)** are build definitions — each is a directory under `rbev-vessels/`: ``` rbev-vessels/ ├── rbev-sentry-debian-slim/ # Sentry vessel (conjure — built from source) │ ├── Dockerfile │ └── rbrv.env ├── rbev-bottle-ifrit/ # Ifrit bottle vessel (conjure — built from source) │ ├── Dockerfile │ └── rbrv.env └── rbev-bottle-plantuml/ # PlantUML server (bind — upstream image pinned by digest) └── rbrv.env ``` [Conjure](#Conjure) [Vessels](#Vessel) have a Dockerfile and are built by Cloud Build. [Bind](#Bind) [Vessels](#Vessel) (like `rbev-bottle-plantuml`) pin an external image by digest in `rbrv.env` — no Dockerfile, no build step. [Graft](#Graft) [Vessels](#Vessel) push a locally-built image to GAR via docker push — no Cloud Build for the image, but about and [Vouch](#Vouch) still run in Cloud Build. The same [Ordain](#Ordain) operation handles all three: it detects the [Vessel](#Vessel) mode and triggers a Cloud Build ([Conjure](#Conjure)), mirrors from upstream ([Bind](#Bind)), or pushes a local image ([Graft](#Graft)). Trust hierarchy: [Conjure](#Conjure) has full SLSA provenance, [Bind](#Bind) has digest-pin verification, [Graft](#Graft) has no provenance chain (GRAFTED verdict). **[Nameplates](#Nameplate)** tie [Vessels](#Vessel) together into a runnable [Crucible](#Crucible). The [Nameplate](#Nameplate) moniker (e.g. `tadmor`) identifies the unit across all operations: ``` .rbk/tadmor/rbrn.env # Maps tadmor → rbev-sentry-debian-slim + rbev-bottle-ifrit ``` [Charging](#Charge) `tadmor` starts the [Crucible](#Crucible) defined by the `tadmor` [Nameplate](#Nameplate), which selects its [Sentry](#Sentry) and [Bottle](#Bottle) [Vessel](#Vessel) images. ## Testing Run test fixtures sequentially — they share regime state and container namespaces. Never run fixtures in parallel. Two qualification gates exercise the full system: a fast qualify checks tabtarget structure, colophon integrity, and [Nameplate](#Nameplate) health; a release qualify adds shellcheck analysis and the complete test suite. ## Recovery - **Lost OAuth credentials**: Download a fresh JSON key from Google Cloud Console and re-run the [Payor](#Payor) installation - **Expired tokens**: Run the [Payor](#Payor) refresh operation - **Compromised [Governor](#Governor)**: Re-mantle the [Governor](#Governor) (replaces the service account, invalidates the old credential) - **Compromised [Director](#Director)/[Retriever](#Retriever)**: Forfeit the compromised account, then re-[Knight](#Knight) or re-[Charter](#Charter) - **Lost [Nameplate](#Nameplate) values**: Re-[Tally](#Tally) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) to retrieve values from the registry - **Build timeout or failure**: [Tally](#Tally) to check build status, review logs in the GCP Console for the [Depot](#Depot) project ## Architecture ``` Project Root/ ├── .buk/ # BUK launcher directory + BURC config ├── .rbk/ # Recipe Bottle config regimes ├── tt/ # TabTargets — all CLI commands live here ├── Tools/ │ ├── buk/ # Bash Utility Kit (portable CLI infrastructure) │ └── rbk/ # Recipe Bottle Kit (domain logic) └── rbev-vessels/ # Vessel definitions (Dockerfile + rbrv.env per vessel) ``` ## Claude Code If you use [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code), the project includes a `CLAUDE.md` with a full command reference table, glossary, and conventions for AI-assisted development. ## License Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc. Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0.

content.text:
Good question. The current README has 25 Foundry definitions and 7 Crucible definitions, organized roughly by conceptual grouping but without a clear learning path. Let me sketch what I see, then we can discuss.

**Current order** (Foundry section): Foundry → Vessel → Hallmark → Vouch → Depot → Ordain → Conjure/Bind/Graft → Kludge → Enshrine → Reliquary → Pouch → Tethered/Airgap → Summon → Plumb → Tally → Levy → Payor → Governor → Director → Retriever → Charter → Knight

The issue: you hit "Vessel" and "Hallmark" before you know what a "Depot" is or who the roles are. The definitions reference each other forward — you're constantly reading terms you haven't met yet.

**A pedagogical ordering might look like:**

**Foundry section** — tell the story in the order someone would encounter it:

1. **Roles first** (who are the actors): Payor → Governor → Director → Retriever → Knight → Charter
2. **Infrastructure** (what do they operate on): Depot → Levy → Regime
3. **Build definitions** (what gets built): Vessel → Conjure/Bind/Graft → Tethered/Airgap
4. **Build machinery** (how it gets built): Enshrine → Reliquary → Pouch → Kludge → Ordain
5. **Provenance lifecycle** (what comes out): Hallmark → Tally → Vouch → Summon → Plumb

**Crucible section** — similar principle:

1. **The problem** (what you're protecting against): Bottle
2. **The apparatus** (how it works): Sentry → Pentacle → Crucible → Nameplate
3. **Lifecycle** (operating it): Charge → Quench

The core question: do you want readers to learn *who does what* before *what the things are*, or do you prefer a different entry point? There's a tension between "roles first" (organizational clarity) and "things first" (concrete before abstract). The current order leans "things first" but jumps around within that.

What's your instinct on the entry point?

message.content:
Foundry: I think Depot first followed closely by role nouns.  Then build definitions.  An interesting choice we could make is to 'demote' verb definitions in some (/ many?) cases to be the sentence where the idea is introduced under a 'noun' section.  Thoughts?

Maybe similar idea for Crucible.

content.text: I like this a lot. It would cut the heading count roughly in half and turn the glossary from a dictionary into a story. **The demotion test**: a verb earns its own heading only if it has independent complexity a reader needs to find. Otherwise it becomes a bolded inline definition within the noun that gives it meaning. Applying that: **Foundry — headings that survive:** - Depot (absorbs Levy) - Payor (absorbs its OAuth/establishment narrative) - Governor (absorbs Knight, Charter as "what the Governor does") - Director (absorbs Ordain, Tally, Vouch — they're the Director's workflow) - Retriever (absorbs Summon, Plumb) - Vessel (absorbs Conjure/Bind/Graft as modes, absorbs Tethered/Airgap as egress modes, absorbs Kludge as dev workflow) - Hallmark (absorbs nothing — it's the output, referenced from Director's verbs) - Reliquary, Pouch, Enshrine — judgment call. Enshrine could fold into Depot or Reliquary. **Crucible — headings that survive:** - Crucible (absorbs Charge, Quench) - Sentry - Pentacle - Bottle - Nameplate **The anchor question** is the main mechanical concern. Onboarding has 27 `buh_tlt` link targets. Demoted verbs still need `` inline where the term is introduced, so the links still resolve. That's straightforward — just moves the anchor from the heading to the sentence. **What I'd want your read on**: Hallmark feels like it could go either way. It's a noun, but its meaning is mostly realized through Director verbs (Ordain creates one, Tally inventories them, Vouch attests them). Does Hallmark keep its own heading, or does it fold into Director as "what the Director produces"?

message.content: Hallmark needs a heading because it is the identity for a whole build.  the next subtlety I am musing is whether we ought to split 'introduction of key noun anchors with inline verb anchors' into 'key noun anchor definition' and 'stories tying nouns to inline verb anchors.'  Thoughts?  Is that a clear question?

content.text:
Clear question. You're asking whether to separate **what things are** from **how things interact**.

Option A (merged): Each noun heading defines itself AND tells the story of its verbs inline. Depot's section both says "a GCP project with a registry" and narrates "the Payor *levies* a Depot, provisioning..."

Option B (split): First pass defines all the nouns cleanly — short, self-contained, no forward references. Second pass tells the workflow stories that tie nouns together through verbs. "The Payor levies a Depot. The Governor knights a Director within it. The Director ordains Hallmarks..."

Option B has a real advantage: the noun definitions become **stable reference targets** that don't need to carry narrative weight. A reader clicking a `buh_tlt` link lands on a clean definition, not mid-paragraph in a workflow story. And the workflow stories can assume the reader has met all the nouns, so they flow without forward-reference anxiety.

The risk with B is repetition — you'd restate nouns in the story section that were just defined above. But that might actually be pedagogically *good*: first encounter is "what is this thing," second encounter is "watch it move."

The structural question is whether the stories section is one continuous narrative or organized around something. Candidates:
- **By role**: "The Payor's journey," "The Director's journey" — natural but creates four mini-stories
- **By lifecycle**: setup → build → verify → consume — one story, chronological
- **By domain**: Foundry workflow, then Crucible workflow — matches the existing two-domain split

My instinct: lifecycle within each domain. Foundry gets one narrative (provision → build → verify → distribute), Crucible gets one (configure → charge → use → quench). What's yours?

message.content: I like the lifecycle idea.  Okay, any more questions to answer before we attempt this?

content.text:
A few things to nail down:

1. **Enshrine, Reliquary, Pouch** — these are nouns but specialized infrastructure. Do they get their own headings, or fold into Depot (Enshrine) and Vessel/Director (Reliquary, Pouch)?

2. **Foundry/Crucible umbrella definitions** — do they stay as heading-level definitions, or become the intro paragraph for their section (no separate definition block)?

3. **Overlap with downstream sections** — "How It Works," "Setup," and "Day-to-Day Operations" already tell workflow stories. Once the definitions section has its own lifecycle narrative, those sections may become partly redundant. Trim them, merge them into the new structure, or leave them alone for now?

4. **Tethered/Airgap** — fold into Vessel as egress modes, or fold into the Foundry lifecycle story as a "when you set up your build pipeline" detail?

message.content:
1) I doubt your comprehension: Enshrine is a verb.  Can definitely be kept in a 'lifecycle story section'.  Reliquary is also a noun but can be defined in the 'lifecycle story' section too, same as Pouch

2) Excellent to merge them with their heading level: one landing spot

3) Merge into new structure if you can.  We can have 'variant lifecycle stages' if that helps.

4) Lets try putting Tethered/Airgap as anchors defined inline under 'depot', phrased as profiles clearly explaining the Depot breadth.  Then we can link to them in Vessel detail descriptions.

More questions?

content.text:
Fair correction on Enshrine — verb, not noun. Got it.

One more question: **Regime**. It currently lives down in the Configuration section with its own heading and anchor. It's a cross-cutting noun (both domains use it). Does it:

- Stay where it is in Configuration (separate from both Foundry and Crucible)
- Move up as a brief definition before the two domain sections (a "shared vocabulary" preamble)
- Get defined inline the first time it's referenced in a lifecycle story

My instinct is it stays in Configuration — it's infrastructure vocabulary, not domain vocabulary. But it does appear in Foundry definitions (Nameplate regime files, etc.) so readers will hit the term before they reach that section.

Anything else I should know about voice or length targets before I draft?
